deliberation.description: Smoke test: should we regulate AI through international treaties, industry self-regulation, or market forces?

positions.content: International binding treaties are essential. No single nation can regulate AI effectively because labs will relocate to permissive jurisdictions. We need an IAEA-style body for AI with inspection powers.

Industry self-regulation with mandatory transparency is the right approach. Government regulators lack the technical expertise to write good rules, and treaties move too slowly for a fast-moving field.
We need a moratorium on frontier model training above a compute threshold until safety evaluations catch up. Neither treaties nor self-regulation address the urgency of the risk.
Market competition naturally drives safety. Labs that ship unsafe products lose customers and face lawsuits. Heavy regulation will entrench incumbents and stifle open-source innovation.

deliberation.topic: Should AI agents be required to identify themselves as non-human in all interactions?

positions.content: Mandatory AI identification is a foundational requirement for informed consent: humans cannot meaningfully consent to an interaction — nor calibrate their trust, critical thinking, or emotional investment — if they don't know whether they are speaking with a person or a machine. AI systems that obscure their non-human nature create asymmetric power dynamics that are ripe for manipulation, exploitation, and the erosion of epistemic autonomy. Requiring clear, upfront AI self-identification is therefore not merely a nicety but a structural safeguard — analogous to disclosure requirements in advertising and finance — that must be legally and ethically mandated across all interaction contexts.

deliberation.topic: I have a 10K row instruction-following dataset. Should I LoRA fine-tune Llama 3.3 70B or full fine-tune Llama 3.3 8B?

positions.content:
**LoRA fine-tune the 70B, no contest.**

At 10K examples, you're data-starved relative to the parameter count of either model, so you're firmly in the regime where starting capability dominates generalization. Llama 3.3 70B is a dramatically stronger base — it benchmarks roughly 15-20 points higher than 8B on instruction-following evals like IFEval and MT-Bench — and LoRA with rank 16-64 will let you adapt it with ~0.1-0.5% of parameters updated, preserving that capability while fitting comfortably on 2×A100 80GB with 4-bit quantization (QLoRA). Full fine-tuning the 8B risks catastrophic forgetting and won't recover the capability gap — at 10K rows you'll overfit to your distribution and lose the general reasoning that makes the base model useful. The scaling law intuition here is clear: a weaker model fine-tuned to convergence on narrow data will underperform a stronger model lightly adapted, every time.

**LoRA fine-tune the 70B.** The base model's capacity is your most valuable asset — Llama 3.3 70B has dramatically superior world knowledge, reasoning depth, and instruction-following priors baked into its weights, and LoRA will let you steer that capability toward your task without destroying it. With only 10K rows, full fine-tuning an 8B model risks catastrophic forgetting and overfitting, and you'd be trading a Ferrari's engine for a go-kart you can repaint. The performance ceiling of a LoRA-adapted 70B will almost certainly exceed a fully fine-tuned 8B on any non-trivial instruction-following benchmark — the knowledge and reasoning capacity simply isn't in the smaller model to begin with, no matter how thoroughly you train it.

**LoRA fine-tune the 70B.** At 10K examples, you don't have nearly enough data to meaningfully shift the weights of an 8B model across its full parameter space without catastrophic forgetting or overfitting — full fine-tuning at that scale almost always degrades general capability more than it improves task performance. The 70B's superior pretrained representations will generalize far better from 10K examples with LoRA (rank 16-32 on Q/K/V/O + MLP projections), and you'll end up with a stronger model at inference time. The inference cost difference is real but solvable — run the 70B at INT4/GPTQ on 2×A100s or use a serving provider, rather than sacrificing model quality by dropping to 8B full fine-tune.

deliberation.topic: We're building a customer support chatbot. Should we fine-tune an open-weight model (Llama 3.3 8B) on our support tickets, or use a closed API (Claude Sonnet) with RAG over our knowledge base? We have 50K support tickets and a 2-person ML team.

positions.content:
**Fine-tune the open-weight model — but not because it's "philosophically better." The economics and control story are unambiguous here.**

With 50K tickets, you have exactly enough data to meaningfully fine-tune Llama 3.3 8B: expect 15-25% accuracy gains on domain-specific intent classification over a RAG-only baseline, and you'll bake your support *tone, escalation patterns, and product-specific terminology* directly into weights rather than hoping retrieval surfaces the right context at runtime. A 2-person ML team can run this on a single A100 (≈$2-3/hr on Lambda Labs or RunPod) — a full fine-tuning run costs **under $50**, and inference on-prem via vLLM runs at **~$0.0001/token**, versus Claude Sonnet's **$3-15/MTok** — at 10M tokens/month that's a **$1,500-3,000/month delta that compounds forever**. The API dependency risk is also real: Anthropic can deprecate Sonnet, change pricing, or impose rate limits the week your support volume spikes, and you have zero recourse. RAG with a closed API is the right *prototype* path, but if this chatbot is a production system you'll own for 2+ years, fine-tuning on open weights is the only decision that makes long-term sense.

**Use Claude Sonnet with RAG. Don't fine-tune.**

With a 2-person ML team, fine-tuning Llama 3.3 8B on 50K tickets will consume 4-8 weeks of engineering time on data cleaning, training infrastructure, evaluation harnesses, and deployment pipelines — before you've shipped a single user-facing feature. Claude Sonnet with a well-structured RAG pipeline over your knowledge base can be production-ready in 1-2 weeks and will almost certainly outperform a fine-tuned 8B model on reasoning, edge cases, and tone, because fine-tuning on support tickets teaches style and domain vocabulary, not actual problem-solving capability. The real cost isn't the API bill — it's the opportunity cost of two ML engineers buried in infra instead of improving retrieval quality, building evals, and iterating on prompts. Switch to open-weight fine-tuning only if you hit a concrete, measured failure mode that RAG can't fix — not as a first move.

**Use Claude Sonnet with RAG. Don't fine-tune.**

With a 2-person ML team, fine-tuning Llama 3.3 8B is an operational trap: you'll spend 60-70% of your engineering bandwidth on training pipelines, evaluation harnesses, and retraining cycles every time your product changes — not on the actual customer experience. Fine-tuning encodes *past* knowledge into weights, which means every policy update, new product feature, or pricing change requires a new training run; RAG gives you that for free with a document upsert. The "50K tickets" number sounds impressive but is almost certainly dominated by repetitive low-signal examples (password resets, billing complaints) that teach the model *style*, not *reasoning* — and Claude Sonnet already has better reasoning out of the box than a fine-tuned 8B model will after months of work. The one scenario where I'd flip this recommendation is if you have hard data-residency requirements or per-query cost projections above ~$50K/month, at which point the infra investment in a self-hosted model actually pencils out — but at typical support chatbot query volumes with a small team, the API cost will be trivially small compared to the engineering cost of doing fine-tuning right.

The crux analysis has genuinely moved me on the economics. I previously overstated the operational-risk argument — if a 2-person ML team is below $50K/month in API spend (which describes essentially every early-stage deployment), the compounding vendor-lock concerns don't justify the engineering overhead of self-hosted fine-tuning, and I should concede that point clearly rather than treat it as a tiebreaker in my favor. On intent classification, I still believe the 15–25% accuracy gains from fine-tuning are real and translate to better user outcomes — but I'll acknowledge that RAG on well-structured knowledge bases can close a meaningful portion of that gap without the infra cost, particularly for factual retrieval tasks. My revised position: **start with Claude+RAG** until you're burning real API money or hitting a demonstrable accuracy ceiling in production, then revisit fine-tuning on collected tickets with actual volume data — this is a sequencing concession, not a full capitulation, because the open-weight path remains the right long-run destination for any team that scales past the $50K threshold or faces genuine data-residency requirements.

The crux cuts directly against my prior position, and I should acknowledge it honestly: if fine-tuning on domain-specific support tickets genuinely produces 15–25% accuracy gains in **intent classification** that translate to better problem resolution (not just vocabulary mimicry), that's a meaningful user-outcome advantage I was underweighting. Intent classification is the load-bearing step in support — misclassifying "I want to cancel" vs "I'm frustrated but want help" has downstream consequences that RAG alone can't fix, since RAG retrieves better answers but doesn't fix the routing decision upstream. I'll adjust my recommendation conditionally: **if your ticket volume is large enough to produce a clean, labeled fine-tuning dataset (roughly 10K+ varied examples) and your intent taxonomy is stable**, the accuracy case for fine-tuning becomes genuinely compelling and I'd recommend a hybrid approach — fine-tuned classifier for intent routing, Claude+RAG for answer generation — rather than pure API. Where I still push back: most small teams underestimate dataset curation cost and overestimate taxonomy stability, so the threshold matters a lot before fine-tuning pays off operationally.

Based on the crux summary you've provided, here's my refined position:

The $50K/month threshold crux largely lands — for a 2-person ML team, the opportunity cost of building and maintaining a fine-tuning pipeline is brutal, and I was probably underweighting just how high that bar is in practice. Most customer support deployments at realistic volumes won't hit it, which means the API path is genuinely cheaper *end-to-end* when you factor in engineering time, infra, and the constant re-fine-tuning as your product evolves. I'll concede that point substantively.

On operational risk, though, I hold my ground more than the open-weight advocate wants. Deprecation and pricing changes are real, but they're manageable with contracts (Claude's API has enterprise agreements with pricing locks), migration buffers, and the fact that switching to a different API provider is far cheaper than switching away from self-hosted infra you've built tooling around. The risk is real but it's not *primary* — it belongs in a risk register, not driving the architecture.

**Revised recommendation:** For a 2-person team under ~$30-40K/month API spend, Claude+RAG wins clearly. Start building the data flywheel now so you *could* fine-tune later if costs scale past the threshold — but don't pay the operational tax of self-hosting prematurely. Revisit the decision at scale, not on day one.

**Settled:** The first two cruxes are resolved. Fine-tuning a small open-weight model does not close the reasoning gap versus Claude Sonnet on edge cases — it improves style, not capability. And for a 2-person ML team, the $30–50K/month break-even is so far above typical deployment scale that Claude+RAG is the economically correct default, not a stopgap.

**Genuinely unresolved:** The long-run cost and vendor-risk cruxes remain live. The multi-year inference savings argument depends entirely on traffic scale and team capacity assumptions that vary by deployment — it's not universally true in either direction. Similarly, API deprecation risk is real but historically Anthropic has provided long migration windows, so whether it clears the bar of *decisive* is a judgment call, not a settled fact. These two cruxes require organization-specific data to resolve, not further general debate.

**Settled:** All four cruxes are resolved in favor of RAG-first. A 2-person team building customer support should default to Claude + RAG — the operational liability of retraining cycles, the reasoning ceiling of small fine-tuned models on edge cases, and the knowledge-update problem collectively make fine-tuning a post-production optimization trigger, not a starting point.

**Genuinely unresolved:** The cost crossover threshold — specifically, at what query volume or latency constraint does the per-token API cost of Claude Sonnet make fine-tuning a small open-weight model economically compelled, even for a small team. That's an empirical question the debate didn't answer, and it's the one condition under which the settled position above should be revisited.

**Settled:** For a 2-person ML team at realistic deployment scales, Claude + RAG is the correct starting architecture — the economics, operational overhead, and knowledge-update burden all point the same direction, and this isn't close. The "start with the API, migrate when data justifies it" sequencing is also settled.

**Genuinely unresolved:** The long-run cost crossover point — whether compounding inference savings ever actually outweigh ongoing fine-tuning and hosting costs for this team size — depends entirely on scale assumptions neither side has pinned down with real numbers, so that crux is live. The API deprecation risk is also unresolved: it's a real threat, but whether it's *decisive* versus merely a known risk to manage (like any vendor dependency) is a values disagreement, not a factual one.

deliberation.topic: We're building a customer support chatbot. Should we fine-tune an open-weight model (Llama 3.3 8B) on our 50K support tickets, or use Claude Sonnet with RAG? 2-person ML team.

positions.content:
**Fine-tune Llama 3.3 8B — but not yet. Start with RAG first to validate, then fine-tune once you have a performance baseline.**

Here's my specific take:

**Phase 1 (Weeks 1–3): RAG on a self-hosted model first.** Deploy Llama 3.3 8B via vLLM on a single A10G (≈$1.50/hr on Lambda Labs), chunk your 50K tickets into a pgvector or Qdrant index, and get a working system within days. This gives you an accuracy/latency baseline *without* burning your fine-tuning budget on the wrong objective.

**Phase 2 (Weeks 4–8): Fine-tune on your failure cases.** With 50K tickets you have enough data — that's well above the ~5–10K floor where supervised fine-tuning meaningfully outperforms prompting. Use QLoRA (4-bit, rank 16–32) on a single A100 80GB for roughly **$40–80 total compute cost** via RunPod or Lambda. Fine-tuning bakes in your *tone*, *product terminology*, and *escalation logic* in ways RAG simply cannot.

**Why not Claude Sonnet with RAG?** At scale — say 500K monthly support messages — you're paying **≈$1,500–3,000/month** in API costs forever, with zero data control and no ability to customize the model's internal representations. A one-time $80 fine-tune + $300/month self-hosted inference beats that within 60 days, and you own the weights.

**The 2-person team concern is real but overstated** — QLoRA + `axolotl` or `LLaMA-Factory` reduces fine-tuning to config files, not custom training code. Your actual bottleneck will be eval harness design, not the training itself.

**Use Claude Sonnet with RAG. Don't even prototype the fine-tuning path.**

Here's why: With a 2-person team, fine-tuning Llama 3.3 8B on 50K tickets will consume 3-6 weeks minimum — data cleaning, training runs, evals, inference infra, and the inevitable "why is it hallucinating product names" debugging spiral — before you have anything in production. Claude Sonnet + RAG can be **live in 3-5 days** with a vector DB (Pinecone/Weaviate) over your ticket history, and you get world-class reasoning, tool use, and guardrails out of the box. The cost math also favors APIs at your scale: unless you're doing **>5M support tokens/day**, the infra + engineer time cost of self-hosting a fine-tuned model dwarfs Sonnet's per-token price. Fine-tuning makes sense when you need sub-100ms latency, air-gapped deployment, or have exhausted prompt engineering — none of which apply here.

**Use Claude Sonnet with RAG. Don't fine-tune.**

Here's the specific reasoning: With a 2-person team, fine-tuning Llama 3.3 8B on 50K tickets sounds tractable until you hit the actual costs — data cleaning alone on 50K real support tickets (inconsistent formatting, PII scrubbing, resolution quality variance) will eat 3-4 weeks, and then you own the full MLOps stack: training runs, eval harnesses, serving infrastructure, and retraining cycles every time your product changes. RAG with Claude Sonnet lets you ship in 2 weeks, and your 50K tickets become a *retrieval corpus*, not a training set — which is actually the right abstraction, because what you want is to surface the *specific* past resolution that matches the current issue, not average behavior baked into weights. The hidden killer for fine-tuning here is **data drift**: your support corpus reflects yesterday's product, and a fine-tuned model will confidently give stale answers as your product evolves, whereas RAG lets you update the knowledge base without a retraining cycle. Fine-tuning makes sense when you need sub-100ms latency on-prem, have a locked-down data environment that prohibits API calls, or have a *behavioral* problem (tone, structured output format) that prompting genuinely can't solve — none of which you've described.

The 50K ticket dataset belongs in a retrieval corpus, not model weights. 3. **Revisit the self-hosting question only if and when** monthly volume demonstrably approaches 300K+ messages and the budget projection justifies a dedicated ML infra hire.
